description: The IACS shall support the capability to segment the network into security zones and conduits to limit unauthorized communications.
The IACS shall protect the zone boundary by monitoring and controlling inter-zone communications.
The IACS shall uniquely identify and authenticate all human users. Default credentials are a violation of this requirement.
The IACS shall protect the confidentiality of information in transit. Cleartext OT protocols expose commands and process values.
The IACS shall provide the capability to verify the intended operation of security functions. ATT&CK findings indicate active threats requiring verification.
All devices in the IACS shall be identified. Passive network discovery provides device inventory evidence.
Devices shall be assigned to appropriate Purdue Model zones. This enables proper zone and conduit design.
The IACS shall provide the capability to monitor and control all methods of access via untrusted networks including remote access.
The IACS shall provide the capability to operate in a degraded mode during a DoS event. Redundancy protocols (MRP/RSTP/HSR/PRP) provide evidence of DoS protection measures.
The ICS network shall be segmented to limit the spread of incidents and enforce least-privilege connectivity. A flat network architecture violates this principle.
Remote access to the ICS shall be strictly controlled and monitored. All remote access paths shall be identified and authorized.
Default accounts and credentials shall be changed before deployment. Factory-default credentials represent a significant account management failure.
Sensitive ICS data in transit shall be protected using encryption where technically feasible. Cleartext OT protocols expose process data and control commands.
A complete and accurate asset inventory shall be maintained for all ICS components. Passive network discovery provides the foundational inventory.
The organization shall detect, contain, and recover from ICS security incidents. ATT&CK findings indicate detectable adversary activity requiring incident response.
Identify and categorize all BES Cyber Systems and their associated Electronic Access Points. Device inventory from passive network discovery provides identification evidence.
Identify and protect all Electronic Security Perimeters. A flat network without segmentation makes perimeter definition impossible.
Control and monitor interactive remote access to BES Cyber Systems. All remote access paths must be identified and authorized.
Protect BES Cyber System Information in transit and at rest. Cleartext protocols expose operational data and control signals.
Disable or restrict logical network ports and services not required for operations. Internet-exposed ICS devices violate this requirement.
Manage system access including default passwords. Default credentials on BES Cyber Systems are a direct CIP-007 violation.
Develop and maintain a baseline configuration for BES Cyber Systems. ATT&CK findings may indicate unauthorized configuration changes.
